meta.title: Voluspa (Prophecy of the Seeress)
meta.provenance: Iceland, oral tradition from ~900 CE; first written ~1270 CE
meta.notes: Voluspa is the first and most important poem in the Poetic Edda. It narrates creation, the first war, the fates of gods, and culminates in Ragnarok (world destruction by fire and flood) followed by world rebirth. Key eschatological stanzas: 40-53 (Ragnarok), 54-66 (renewal). Baldr returns (st.57), a righteous hall (Gimlé) appears (st.59). Old Norse text included for key stanzas (1-3, 41, 52, 54, 59).

1:1.text:
Hearing I ask | from the holy races,
From Heimdall's sons, | both high and low;
Thou wilt, Valfather, | that well I relate
Old tales I remember | of men long ago.
1:1.text_on:
Hljóðs bið ek allar
helgar kindir,
meiri ok minni
mögu Heimdallar;
viltu, at ek, Valföðr,
vel fram telja
forn spjöll fira,
þau er fremst um man.

1:2.text:
I remember yet | the giants of yore,
Who gave me bread | in the days gone by;
Nine worlds I knew, | the nine in the tree
With mighty roots | beneath the mold.
1:2.text_on:
Ek man jötna
ár um borna,
þá er forðum mik
fœdda höfðu;
níu man ek heima,
níu íviðjur,
mjötvið mœran
fyr mold neðan.

1:3.text_on:
Ár var alda
þar er Ymir bygði,
var-a sandr né sær
né svalar unnir;
jörð fannsk æva
né upphiminn,
gap var Ginnunga
en gras hvergi.

1:4.text:
Then Bur's sons lifted | the level land,
Mithgarth the mighty | there they made;
The sun from the south | warmed the stones of earth,
And green was the ground | with growing leeks.

1:5.text:
The sun, the sister | of the moon, from the south
Her right hand cast | over heaven's rim;
No knowledge she had | where her home should be,
The moon knew not | what might was his,
The stars knew not | where their stations were.

1:6.text:
Then sought the gods | their assembly-seats,
The holy ones, | and council held;
Names then gave they | to noon and twilight,
Morning they named, | and the waning moon,
Night and evening, | the years to number.

1:7.text:
At Ithavoll met | the mighty gods,
Shrines and temples | they timbered high;
Forges they set, | and they smithied ore,
Tongs they wrought, | and tools they fashioned.

1:8.text:
In their dwellings at peace | they played at tables,
Of gold no lack | did the gods then know,—
Till thither came | up giant-maids three,
Alo powerful, | out of Jotunheim.

1:9.text:
Then sought the gods | their assembly-seats,
The holy ones, | and council held,
To find who should raise | the race of dwarfs
Out of Brimir's blood | and the legs of Blain.

1:10.text:
There was Motsognir | the mightiest made
Of all the dwarfs, | and Durin next;
Many a likeness | of men they made,
The dwarfs in the earth, | as Durin said.

1:11.text:
Nyi and Nithi, | Northri and Suthri,
Austri and Vestri, | Althjof, Dvalin,
Nar and Nain, | Niping, Dain,
Bifur, Bofur, | Bombur, Nori,
An and Onar, | Ai, Mjothvitnir.

1:12.text:
Vigg and Gandalf | Vindalf, Thorin,
Thror and Thrain, | Thekkur, Litur,
Vitr, Nyr, | Nyrath,—now have I told—
Regin and Rathsvith— | the right ones both.

1:13.text:
Fili, Kili, | Fundin, Nali,
Heptifili, | Hannar, Sviur,
Frar, Hornbori, | Fraeg and Loni,
Aurvang, Jari, | Eikinskjaldi.

1:14.text:
The race of the dwarfs | in Dvalin's throng
Down to Lofar | the list must I tell;
The rocks they left, | and through wet lands
To the sands of Joruvall | the way they went.

1:15.text:
There were Draupnir | and Dolgthrasir,
Har, Haugspori, | Hlevang, Gloin,
Dori, Ori, | Duf, Andvari,
Skirfir, Virfir, | Skafith, Ai.

1:16.text:
Alf and Yngvi, | Eikinskjaldi,
Fjalarr and Frosti, | Finn and Ginnarr:
That all may know, | the names are told
Of those who sought | Lofar's home.

1:17.text:
Then from the throng | did three come forth,
From the home of the gods, | the mighty and gracious;
Two without fate | on the land they found,
Ask and Embla, | empty of might.

1:18.text:
Soul they had not, | sense they had not,
Heat nor motion, | nor goodly hue;
Soul gave Othin, | sense gave Honir,
Heat gave Lothur | and goodly hue.

1:19.text:
An ash I know, | Yggdrasil its name,
With water white | is the great tree wet;
Thence come the dews | that fall in the dales,
Green by Urth's well | does it ever grow.

1:20.text:
Thence come the maidens | mighty in wisdom,
Three from the dwelling | down under the tree;
Urth is one named, | Verthandi the next,—
On the wood they scored,—and Skuld the third.
Laws they made there, | and life allotted
To the sons of men, | and set their fates.

1:21.text:
The war I remember, | the first in the world,
When the gods with spears | had smitten Gollveig,
And in the hall | of the High One burned her,
Three times burned, | and three times born,
Oft and again, | yet ever she lives.

1:22.text:
Heith they named her | who sought their home,
The wide-seeing witch, | in magic wise;
Minds she bewitched | that were moved by her magic,
To evil women | a joy she was.

1:23.text:
On the host his spear | did Othin hurl,
Then in the world | did war first come;
The wall that girdled | the gods was broken,
And the field by the warlike | Wanes was trodden.

1:24.text:
Then sought the gods | their assembly-seats,
The holy ones, | and council held,
Whether the gods | should tribute give,
Or to all alike | should worship be paid.

1:25.text:
Then sought the gods | their assembly-seats,
The holy ones, | and council held;
To find who with venom | the air had filled,
Or had given Oth's bride | to the giants' brood.

1:26.text:
In swelling rage | then rose up Thor,—
Seldom he sits | when he such things hears,—
And the oaths were broken, | the words and bonds,
The mighty pledges | between them made.

1:27.text:
I know of the horn | of Heimdall, hidden
Under the high-reaching | holy tree;
On it there pours | from Valfather's pledge
A mighty stream: | do you know yet, or what?

1:28.text:
Alone I sat | when the Old One sought me,
The terror of gods, | and gazed in mine eyes:
'What hast thou to ask? | why comest thou hither?
Othin, I know | where thine eye is hidden.'

1:29.text:
I know where Othin's | eye is hidden,
Deep in the wide-famed | well of Mimir;
Mead from the pledge | of Othin each morn
Does Mimir drink: | do you know yet, or what?

1:31.text:
On the host his spear | did Othin hurl,
Then in the world | did war first come;
Broken was the outer | wall of the gods,
And the Wanes came marching | through the breach.

1:32.text:
I see the baleful | Balder's brother
Unlocks his battle-blade; | one night he slays
The other son | of Othin's wife.
Through middle-fields | and forests dark
The mother dwells | with the grief of men.

1:33.text:
I saw there wading | through rivers wild
Treacherous men | and murderers too,
And workers of ill | with the wives of men;
There Nithhogg sucked | the blood of the slain,
And the wolf tore men; | would you know yet more?

1:34.text:
Hrungnir's slayer | I saw his home,
For the son of the giant, | and the fire of the slain;
Balmunder there | the son of Nori, a-roaming,
A hall he had | beset with serpents.

1:36.text:
In the east there sat | the old one, in Iron-Wood,
And there gave birth | to the brood of Fenrir;
Among these one | in monster's guise
Was soon to steal | the sun from the sky.

1:37.text:
There feeds he full | on the flesh of the dead,
And the home of the gods | he reddens with gore;
Dark grows the sun, | and in summer soon
Come mighty storms: | would you know yet more?

1:38.text:
On a hill there sat, | and smote on his harp,
Eggther the joyous, | the giants' warder;
Above him the cock | in the bird-wood crowed,
Fair and red | did Fjalar stand.

1:39.text:
Then to the gods | crowed Gollinkambi,
He wakes the heroes | in Othin's hall;
And beneath the earth | does another crow,
The rust-red bird | at the bars of Hel.

1:40.text:
Now Garm howls loud | before Gnipahellir,
The fetters will burst, | and the wolf run free;
Much I know, | and more can see
Of the fate of the gods, | the mighty in battle.

1:41.text_on:
Munu menn ok konur
sakar fella,
ok systrungar
sifjar spilla;
hart er í heimi,
hórdómr mikill,
skeggjöld, skálmöld,
skjöldr klofinn,
vindsöld, vargöld,
áðr verold steypisk;
mun engi maðr
öðrum þyrma.

1:42.text:
Fast move the sons | of Mim, and fate
Is heard in the note | of the Gjallarhorn;
Loud blows Heimdall, | the horn is aloft,
In fear quake all | who on Hel-roads are.

1:43.text:
Yggdrasil shakes, | and shiver on high
The ancient limbs, | and the giant is loose;
To the head of Mim | does Othin give heed,
But the kinsman of Surt | shall slay him soon.

1:44.text:
How fare the gods? | how fare the elves?
All Jotunheim groans, | the gods are in council;
Loud roar the dwarfs | by the doors of stone,
The masters of the rocks: | would you know yet more?

1:45.text:
Now Garm howls loud | before Gnipahellir,
The fetters will burst, | and the wolf run free;
Much I know, | and more can see
Of the fate of the gods, | the mighty in battle.

1:47.text:
O'er the sea from the north | there sails a ship
With the people of Hel, | at the helm stands Loki;
After the wolf | do wild men follow,
And with them the brother | of Byleistr goes.

1:48.text:
Surt fares from the south | with the scourge of branches,
The sun of the battle-gods | shone from his sword;
The crags are sundered, | the giant-women sink,
The dead throng Hel-way, | and heaven is cloven.

1:49.text:
Now comes to Hlin | yet another hurt,
When Othin fares | to fight with the wolf,
And Beli's fair slayer | seeks Surt, for the strife
Of Frigg is ended | when the foe she has lost.

1:50.text:
Then comes Sigfather's | mighty son,
Vidarr, to fight | with the foaming wolf;
In the giant's son | does he thrust his sword
Full to the heart: | his father is avenged.

1:51.text:
Hither there comes | the son of Hlothyn,
The bright snake banes, | and the warder of earth;
In anger smites | the warder of Midgarth,—
Forth goes his way, | but falls ere long,
Treads nine paces, | the son of the earth,
Slain by the serpent | of little fear.

1:52.text:
The sun turns black, | earth sinks in the sea,
The hot stars down | from heaven are whirled;
Fierce grows the steam | and the life-feeding flame,
Till fire leaps high | about heaven itself.
1:52.text_on:
Sól tér sortna,
sígr fold í mar,
hverfa af himni
heiðar stjörnur;
geisar eimi
ok aldrnari,
leikr hár hiti
viðr himin sjalfan.

1:53.text:
Now Garm howls loud | before Gnipahellir,
The fetters will burst, | and the wolf run free;
Much I know, | and more can see
Of the fate of the gods, | the mighty in battle.

1:54.text:
Now do I see | the earth anew
Rise all green | from the waves again;
The cataracts fall, | and the eagle flies,
And fish he catches | beneath the cliffs.
1:54.text_on:
Sér hon upp koma
öðru sinni
jörð ór ægi
iðjagræna;
falla forsar,
flýgr örn yfir,
sá er á fjalli
fiska veiðir.

1:55.text:
The gods in Ithavoll | meet together,
Of the terrible girdler | of earth they talk,
And the mighty past | they call to mind,
And the ancient runes | of the Ruler of Gods.

1:56.text:
In wondrous beauty | once again
Shall the golden tables | stand mid the grass,
Which the gods had owned | in the days of old.

1:57.text:
Then fields unsowed | bear ripened fruit,
All ills grow better, | and Baldr comes back;
Baldr and Hoth dwell | in Hropt's battle-plains,
And the mighty gods: | would you know yet more?

1:58.text:
Then Henir wins | the prophetic wand,
And the sons of the brothers | of Tveggi abide
In Vindheim now: | would you know yet more?

1:59.text:
More fair than the sun, | a hall I see,
Roofed with gold, | on Gimill height;
There shall the righteous | rulers dwell,
And happiness ever | there shall they have.
1:59.text_on:
Sér hon sal standa
sólu fegra,
gulls þaktan,
á Gimlé;
þar skulu dyggvar
dróttir byggja
ok um aldrdaga
ynðis njóta.

1:60.text:
Then comes the dark | dragon flying,
Nithhogg up from | the Nithafjoll mountains;
The bodies of men | on his wings he bears,
The serpent bright: | but now must I sink.

1:61.text:
Now do I see | the earth anew
Rise all green | from the waves again;
The cataracts fall, | and the eagle flies,
And fish he catches | beneath the cliffs.

1:62.text:
The gods in Ithavoll | meet together,
Of the terrible girdler | of earth they talk;
And the mighty past | they call to mind,
And Fimbultyr's | ancient runes.

1:63.text:
In wondrous beauty | once again
Shall the golden tables | stand mid the grass,
Which the gods had owned | in the days of old,
Five of them playing | at chess on the plain.

1:64.text:
There dwells a Ruler, | and watches o'er it,
An ancient and potent | one who does not fail;
He rules and distributes, | decides and settles,
All things from his seat | of justice and judgment.

1:65.text:
Then the powerful One, | the Mighty from above,
Comes to rule, who all things governs;
From above he comes | to the judgment of men,
He who brings peace | to the gods and the slain.
